danger_signals.visual_form: Recurring enemy character archetypes (Andore family variants, stage-specific thugs)
danger_signals.meaning: Immediate physical threat requiring combat engagement

danger_signals.visual_form: Unique, visually distinct character models at stage culmination (Won Won, Freddie, Bratken, Philippe, Rolento, Retu)
danger_signals.meaning: Major narrative threat and gameplay difficulty escalation

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy units carrying melee weapons (knives, cleavers in Japanese version); female acrobatic knife-wielders (Mary/Eliza in JP version, Leon/Robert internationally)
danger_signals.meaning: Enhanced combat danger and damage potential

danger_signals.visual_form: Breakable objects (drums, barrels, cars) integrated into stage layouts
danger_signals.meaning: Interactive danger zones that can be destroyed for resources or tactical advantage

safety_signals.visual_form: Food items hidden within breakable drums and barrels
safety_signals.meaning: Resource availability and player recovery opportunity

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible items distributed throughout stages in destructible containers
safety_signals.meaning: Safe resource gathering and score progression

safety_signals.visual_form: Genryusai Doll (invulnerability effect), Guy Doll (extra life)
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary enhanced survivability and progression safety

safety_signals.visual_form: Tonfa, lumber piece, and knife as stage-placed assets
safety_signals.meaning: Enhanced player combat capability and tactical advantage

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Diverse recurring enemy archetypes (Andore family variants, stage-specific thugs); regional variants across six global locations
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Gang presence distributed across Hong Kong, France, Holland, England, Italy, and Japan—indicating international criminal network expansion

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Three playable character archetypes: Mike Haggar (professional wrestling techniques), Maki Genryusai (Bushin-ryū Ninjutsu school master), Carlos Miyamoto (Japanese-descent martial artist with sword special move)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Global counter-offensive route spanning Eurasia, suggesting organized rescue mission across territories

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mad Gear Gang resurgence after original defeat
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Recurring enemy archetypes reappear; new boss leader (Retu) introduced; gang presence across multiple international locations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies swarm in stage patterns; boss encounters structure difficulty progression; kidnapping plot device drives stage-by-stage progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Six distinct stage environments across different countries (Hong Kong, France, Holland, England, Italy, Japan); stage-specific enemy variants and boss characters
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must travel through global locations; enemy types and boss strategies vary by regional context

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stage-based time progression (12:00pm Hong Kong → 5:00pm France → 4:00pm Holland → 8:00pm England → 12:00pm Italy → 11:30pm Japan)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Linear progression through stages representing temporal and geographic narrative advancement

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Three playable characters with visually distinct fighting animations and special move executions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player choice of character alters combat approach; each character has unique technical repertoire (wrestling, ninjutsu, sword techniques)

environmental_storytelling.example: Six stages distributed across Hong Kong → France → Holland → England → Italy → Japan, communicating international scope of criminal organization and rescue operation
Progression from noon (Hong Kong) through afternoon/evening to late night (Japan), suggesting geographic movement and temporal narrative advancement
Named boss characters (Won Won, Freddie, Bratken, Philippe, Rolento, Retu) indicate regional gang command structure and territorial organization

Japanese version features weapon-wielding female enemies (Mary/Eliza) and cleaver-wielding Won Won; international versions substitute Leon/Robert and remove cleaver—communicating regional content sensitivity
Three-character roster (Haggar as established authority figure, Maki as young ninja heir, Carlos as foreign ally) communicates multi-faction alliance structure

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Appear throughout multiple stages; represent Mad Gear foot soldier consistency; only returning enemy type from first game, signaling gang continuity
Unique enemy types appear in different locations; only Mary/Eliza knife-wielders (JP) and Leon/Robert (international) serve as stage-variant indicators
Unique visual and behavioral patterns; serve as stage culmination encounters; visually distinct from regular enemy archetypes
Omitted from SNES port of first game but returns in Final Fight 2; signals gang reconstruction and past antagonist revival
Appears in Japan stage; identified as new Mad Gear leader replacing Belger; final encounter signals narrative resolution

notes: Final Fight 2 uses visual differentiation across six geographic stages to communicate international criminal organization scope. Enemy archetypes (particularly Andore family continuity) reinforce gang persistence. Time-of-day progression and boss character naming conventions establish regional hierarchy. Character-specific visual fighting styles (Haggar's wrestling, Maki's ninjutsu, Carlos's sword techniques) communicate tactical diversity. Significant censorship variants between Japanese and international versions affect visual danger signals (weapon types, female enemy presence). The game communicates world-state primarily through geographic progression and enemy type distribution rather than environmental color-coding or symbolic faction markers.
